speaker: interviewer
text: And I do want to know the names of your children.

speaker: interviewer
text: Anytime

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. How did you find out how your-- that your father died?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was an advertisement in the paper one day, 15 years ago, asking the whereabouts of Ellen Kerry Lewis, as I was then, at the address I had lived earlier. This was in the local paper. Would I please, or would anyone knowing about me, please contact J. Wertheim in Melbourne, Australia. So we sent cablegrams. We managed to get the telephone number. And for a week, nothing happened. No answer. There's 24 hours difference in time, or 12 hours, rather. And stayed up each night to phone at night. Until I got so exasperated. The same advertisement came into the paper the week after. And I picked the paper up at 5 o'clock. And I saw this little thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: And picked up the telephone, phoned Australia. And a very sleepy female voice said to me, what do you think you're doing, phoning at three o'clock in the morning?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I said, I've been phoning for seven days, and I'm getting very tired of this. And she suddenly realized who I was. And she said, well, I didn't expect to hear from you so soon. Well, it turned out that she was my father's-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] --third wife, or widow, rather. My father had died five years before. And I still, to this day, don't know why she wanted to contact me. But that's when I discovered he had died. We eventually went out to Australia. And I still didn't learn anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: You didn't?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I don't know why she called me, why she wanted to find me. I don't know. No one knew. Because I met quite a lot of my father's friends. No one knew why he didn't contact me. All they knew was that there was always, my wedding photograph, in wherever he lived. And he always carried a copy of the little photograph that was taken in the orphanage. And I still have that photograph now. It's the only thing of his I have.

speaker: interviewer
text: And let's go back to your marriage then, and your children. If you could tell us when they were born and their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was married in May, '48. Anita, my daughter, was born in March '49. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My son, Martin, was born in 1955. And as I said, I lost two-- had two miscarriages in between. It was a marriage made in hell, in every possible, every conceivable way. And I waited until my daughter was old enough-- she was going to-- she was going to university on the Monday. On the Friday, it was a case of the straw that broke the camel's back. In the March, my son had been bar mitzvah. Because I, again, I kept a kosher home. And always spent my life Orthodox. But that particular day, I could take no more. I was working, as always.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was a service manager in a garage. And the boys, as they were laughingly known, who I got on with very well, kept this old little car going for me. And every Christmas they would put a reconditioned engine in, or a gearbox in. It was very old when I had it. And it lasted-- thank God-- many years after.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I packed what I could into the car. My son was out. And my daughter, as I said, was going to university on-- two days later. And I packed what I could in the car, which wasn't much, and went to a friend.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, I have been very unlucky in most of my life, except in a few things. And the main thing is that I have friends that I have had-- the friend I went to, we have been friends for 52 years. I have another friend, we've been friends for 30-odd years.

speaker: interviewer
text: What are their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: My main friend, her name is Jean Johns. She just opened the door to me and said, it's about time. She made up a bed. I went back to my old home, to get more clothes two days later, only to find my husband had changed the locks. My daughter had gone to university. My son stayed with his father. Because at 13, you're not very sure what you're going to let yourself in for. But only for a little while. I mean, my first husband should never have married. He was a born bachelor. He was born when his mother was 46. And he had grown up, sisters. So he had three mothers. So there was no way he should've married. He wasn't interested in the children. He wasn't interested in anybody except himself. But I stuck it for 20 years, and then no longer. Jean, my friend, had a house. She lived in one house. The next house she also owned, which had been let because Swansea is a-- is a-- has a big university. And these people were just about going. And she made the house into two flats. And I had the bottom flat. I had to share the bathroom and the-- and the kitchen with a Pakistani student. And my son joined me. And we had a living room, one bedroom with twin beds, and a shared kitchen. But it was a very happy place. I wasn't there very long before I became very ill. And I had to have a hysterectomy. My now husband-- and we've been married 24 years-- had been a friend of the family for years and years and years. And his marriage was in even worse condition than mine. But he wasn't going to leave until his children were married. He had two children, Jackie and Paul. And unfortunately, we didn't have any money between us. Because I couldn't work, having just had this miserable operation. So he said, I'm going to leave the house. And we're going to get one together.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he said, when my-- my divorce, of course, had come through, despite my first husband kept-- keeping on saying, there is no way I'm going to divorce you. No way. Happiness for you? Forget it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, he did. And Colin, who is now my husband, decided that we are all going to live together, Martin, my son, and he and I.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we rented this little two-bedroom house. And eventually his divorce came through. And he lives in the most beautiful part of what we call [INAUDIBLE], right on the cliff. And he said to me, do you want to live here? Or shall I sell it and we'll find a completely new home. Oh no, I said, I've always wanted to live here. So we virtually stripped the house and rebuilt the inside. And it was-- Again, and when did-- what was the date that you got married?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I got married on the 18th of March, 1972. So that was 24 years ago. That was second time around, 24 years ago. All in all, with the two marriages, I'll have been married 48 years, and I'm 67. It's not bad going. [LAUGHTER] But that was hard, very hard. But my children were happier, much happier. My daughter did very well. My son, who didn't know what he wanted to do-- my husband was-- and now Colin was in transport, you know, with-- with one of the big car firms. So because Martin didn't know what he wanted to do, we put him into transport with some friends of ours. And he's virtually been in it ever since. And for someone who didn't like schooling, he was-- he went on to become transport manager by the time he was 21. Because he enjoyed it. And it-- it was a-- he always loved a challenge. So he-- he did very well. My daughter's a teacher, has been a teacher for many a moon. And they're both married to the most delightful partners. If I would've had the choice of partners for them, I think I would've picked the ones they have. My Martin is married to Wendy. And Anita is married to Hugh. And Anita and Hugh have given me two wonderful grandchildren. There's Daniel, who's 18. And there's Hannah, who's 13. And that is the only thing I have to thank my first husband for. And that's for my children. Other than that, I mean-- my-- as you, I think, said, my life, my earlier life was pretty-- not much of a life. My first marriage was no life at all. And just work and work and work.

speaker: interviewer
text: Have you been working since, in this marriage? Has your life been better?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we-- we-- I had to originally. Because before the-- my-- Colin's wife would divorce him, we had to keep her. And to do that, I had to work. But I've been an arthritic for 30-odd years. So I had to come away from the garage. Because that's all out in the open.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then I went and I ran what we call an estate agent's office. Which you call Realtors? Real estate.

speaker: interviewee
text: Real estate. Right. I ran an office there for 4 and a 1/2 years. Unfortunately my right arm was paralyzed for a while. And I couldn't quite cope.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I went into an office, in insurance, car insurance. But I didn't-- wasn't very keen on that. Because there were too many females, just sort of, nattering. And I was used to working with men.

speaker: interviewee
text: I got married from there. And to keep working, I went to work for a newspaper. And then one day, I drove home. And we had to reverse into the little garage. And because I've got spondylitis in my neck, I got stuck in the garage with my head facing the other way.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh.

speaker: interviewee
text: My head was there, on my back. So of course, I couldn't move. And it was just a tiny little garage.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I had to wait for my husband and son to come home, to get me out of this. Which, I don't know how they manage to do it. And I was screaming.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, they called this doctor, who called the specialist, who took me into hospital for about the hundredth time. And the specialist said, forget it. No more work for you.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this coincided with-- that was a year after we were married. So I haven't worked since '73. But I have worked since I was 15, without any respite. And now I can't say I'm a lady of leisure. I'm much too busy. And what are the-- you know, how did you pursue the search for your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, that's a lovely story.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Excuse me. [DRINKS] I went to Israel alone. It's something I have always, all my life, wanted to do. I've never money. Anyway, I saved enough to go to Israel. I needed desperately to go to Yad Vashem. Because I really did not know what happened to my family who died. And I thought they would have records there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So for a week we did touring. And then I was on my own. I had made friends with a lady of my own age. And [? Ann ?] and I decided to go on our own to explore. And the first place we went to was Jerusalem, to Yad Vashem.

speaker: interviewee
text: I went through the halls of Yad Vashem. And I could neither speak or see for tears. And my friend put her head inside the door and ran. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] When I sort of got myself together, I went to the records office. And the young man-- relatively young man-- there went through all the records he could find. And he said, he was desperately sorry, but he could find nothing. No name Wertheim.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I came back home. He gave me the address of a tracing agency in Germany. And he said, write to them if you want to know what happened to your family. What year was this? Did you say this was five years ago?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, this is 10 years ago.

speaker: interviewer
text: 10 Years ago.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I came home. I enjoyed Israel thoroughly.

speaker: interviewee
text: In strange ways, because I've never been able to mourn my family. And I stood at the Wailing Wall. And for the first time in all those years, I cried for them. A strange lady who's name-- face I never saw, put her arms around me. And I felt as if my heart must've been made of ice, and it suddenly melted. And for the first time, I was able to mourn my family. I came home. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And for a while, heard nothing. Then I had a letter from the young man who escorted me around the records. On discovery, he was a rabbi in charge of Yad Vashem. And he said, he was desperately sorry, but he could find no trace of my family. But in his searches, he'd found a letter. And in the corner was the name Wertheim, and an address in New York. But the lady had been there 15 years before me. So I thought, you know, this is useless. In the meantime, I had written to the tracing agency. And I think about six months later, I had a copy of the letter I'd had when I was 12 years old, telling me they'd been deported to Riga, and were dead. What I couldn't face at 12, I couldn't face at 50-odd, and I collapsed. I was-- doctors still visit. He came and kept me sedated for three days. And I woke up to find my husband sitting by my side with a cup of tea, which is very English, and an Airmail letter. He said, drink your tea and write to New York. It wasn't as simple as it sounds. I mean, I had been sedated for three days. Anyway, I eventually wrote to New York.

speaker: interviewer
text: And had a letter back in dreadfully broken English. You-- you've heard it spoken, but have you ever seen it written? It's hilarious.

speaker: interviewee
text: But that wasn't the point. This lady's husband was my father's third cousin. And they'd all been brought up in one little village. And this Morris Wertheim, as his name was, had two brothers and two sisters, also in New York. So I decided I was going to New York. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Well, I knew nothing about America. I certainly knew nothing about New York. And this was the following June. I had written to this lady to say I was coming. And she kept on writing, saying, we have no accommodation for you. Oh, I said, that doesn't matter. I'll find it, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] being very blase, and very not knowing. Anyway, I came to New York, met this lady, and all evening the telephone kept ringing. It wasn't because these sisters and brothers of Morris' wanted to speak to me. But me, because I was Julius's daughter. And then the last phone call, a lady kept on shouting at me, saying, you have to come to Los Angeles. I'm your second cousin. Well, I didn't know from second cousins or anything. I only just found third cousins. So I said, I'm very sorry. But there is no way I can come to California. I said, I'm hoping I've got enough money to see me through here. And I've only got two weeks. But she said, hang on a minute.

speaker: interviewer
text: Her husband came on the phone, have got a pen and paper? Yes. Write down these numbers.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he gave me his American Express number. Now he said, I've only just arrived that day. Now, tomorrow, he said, you will phone the airlines and get a flight to-- to Los Angeles. But he said, you can have a week in New York, you know, very kindly.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this is precisely what I did. And I phoned them to say what I was wearing. Because they didn't know me, and I didn't know them. And I got to New York-- oh, to Los Angeles.

speaker: interviewee
text: I had no idea how long trip was going to take. I had no idea of distances. And to find this lady coming towards me, which was like looking in a mirror, 20 years old. She was 20 years older than I. And her name was Toni. And it was just like looking into a mirror.

speaker: interviewer
text: Anyway, I went-- they took me home. She made a cup of tea. And she said to me, have you been in touch with your Uncle David? And I said, well, who's Uncle David? Your father's eldest brother. I said, where is he? Oh, in Miami, she said. She said, what about Erika? Well, Erika's the aunt who's-- who's clothes I used to have to wear. And she too, was in Miami. So Toni picks up the telephone-- Are these some of the relatives that went to South America?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: They didn't?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my father's family--

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: --who went to New York.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we really didn't have anything to do with them, for a very simple explanation. They had come to America about 1936, '37. And my father had pleaded with my grandmother. Because they came with money. He had pleaded with her to send him enough to bring us out, and he would work until he paid it back. And she replied-- and I remember that letter. She replied, not saying I can't, but saying, I won't. And he never forgave her. As far as I was concerned, I had no family on my father's side. Anyway, Toni picked up the telephone. And she knew me as Ellen. And she's talking to Uncle David about Ellen. He doesn't know who Ellen is, because I'm Kerry. I said, try Kerry.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, I had a feeling the phone was going to explode. And this man is in his 80's. And it had happened Erika was there. So the only thing I could here was my Uncle David, whose name actually is Fritz, saying, when are you coming? So once I decided that, you know, there's no way. I've only got a week. I have to go back to Kennedy. This is June. And it was so hot you couldn't breathe. Anyway, I went back home and corresponded with Erika. And in one of her letters, she said, did you know you had family in Cincinnati? I said, I didn't know of anybody.

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother's sister married my father's brother, and they went to the Argentine. Their children came to Cincinnati. So she gave me the address of Sal, the male cousin. And I wrote to him. And I'd already decided I was going to go to uncle-- see Uncle David-- this was for Pesach the following year-- and go to Los Angeles. And I scraped together every penny I had. Anyway, I had written to Sal. Suddenly, the telephone rings. And I pick it up. And this voice said, this is Sal, from Cincinnati. I said, I've just written to you. I know, he said. I've had your later, and I've sent you airmail-- air-- --plane tickets to Erika, because we have a silver wedding and we want you here. So they'd arranged for me to come to Cincinnati for the-- for the anniversary, and to stay for four days. Well, I arrived in Cincinnati to a tumultuous welcome. I don't know how many people were there with a big notice that said 'Kerry,' which suddenly vanished, ended up on the floor. And these people were coming towards me. And I look up, and there's somebody who looks vaguely like me again.

speaker: interviewer
text: Anyway, we-- we got back to Sal's home, and I just sort of had a cup of tea and got myself together. And the door opens. And this lady comes flying in, in tears, saying, you don't remember me? It was only 56-- 60-- 60 years ago.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I've always had a name in my head all those years. 60 years, I've had this name in my head, which has been [? Sonya. ?] The person who I'm talking to was [? Sonya, ?] as a matter of fact, my cousin, Sunny, who remembers me when she was a very small child, and I was that little bit older. And I've been back to Cincinnati many times since. And I feel as if it's my home. And feel as if Sunny is my cousin-- not my cousin, but my sister. And I came here very-- after being very ill this time. I didn't even think I would make the journey. But she's been one of the most patient of people. And she has, as always, made me more than welcome. And this is this sort of love that I get. And I get it from Toni, who-- who writes-- hates light-- writing letters, who writes cards. And you can feel it in a card. I had an operation last September. And Toni wrote me a card saying, I hope [? it'll ?] be better. And she said, I don't know how to show our love.

speaker: interviewer
text: So please accept this as our love. And there was a check inside for a hundred dollars. Because they couldn't express love any other way.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that's-- now, this is my birth family. My own family is-- is terribly important to me, because I haven't got anybody else. But this family is even more important. Because this is my own family. This is-- this is my roots.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, how did you get to the truth of how your mother and your siblings actually died?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm afraid it's a long story. In 1989, the Kindertransport had a reunion in London. And there were 970 people. And there were tables set out with different names of different towns in Germany. And at Kassel's table there were only three of us. One lady who was five years older than me, and one who was four years younger than me. And we started to chat. And of course, I'd never been back to Germany. And the one younger than I had come to non-Jewish people in Scotland, and had never been back to Kassel. But she said she would go back for me, because I couldn't go back, to see what she could find out. She brought me back to book, a commemorative book which Kassel had printed, which gave the names of all my family that died, [? and ?] [? even ?] photographs of two of my brothers. And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] --I decided, eventually, that I must go back and find out exactly what happened. And a friend who lives in Australia came with me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was that? What was the friend's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: His name is [? Ed. ?] Because the two friends from Kassel, one couldn't speak German any more than I could. And the other one still had friends in Germany. So I needed different company. Anyway, we went to Germany. And we ended up in-- in-- in Kassel. Now, through this Scottish friend-- I'm sorry, it gets complicated. Through this Scottish friend, I had come to know a lady in Kassel who was part of the university. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And when I got to Kassel, I had to phone her. And we took her out for a meal, and then tried to work out a way to get to Hof. Which was very difficult, because there was only one bus a day to Hof from Kassel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she had no idea if there were any coming back. So eventually, she insisted that we take her car. Now, I've never driven on the wrong side of the road, as you do now.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I-- because of my arthritis, I drive an automatic little car on the left side of the road. I'm now in a car that is not mine, which makes me nervous, which is manual, which I can't use my arms properly.

speaker: interviewee
text: And which is driving the wrong side of the road. And I have to get to Hof. I did it with great trepidation. I did it. And then we had to go from there, because as I said, I don't speak German at all. I can understand a little.
